stem: A 38-year-old woman with BPD is on quetiapine 200 mg nocte, sertraline 150 mg daily, and diazepam 5 mg TDS. She has been stable in DBT for 12 months, depression in remission 8 months, no self-harm for 10 months. She asks about medication review.

data_table.Medications: Quetiapine 200 mg nocte, sertraline 150 mg daily, diazepam 5 mg TDS
data_table.History: BPD, MDD in remission 8 months, no self-harm 10 months, stable in DBT 12 months

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medication management approach?

options.text: Continue all unchanged as the patient is stable and change risks destabilisation at this stage
options.rationale: Continuing polypharmacy without review contradicts evidence. No medication has long-term BPD benefit. The NHMRC guideline recommends against polypharmacy. Current stability is likely from DBT. Benzodiazepines are specifically cautioned. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; RANZCP)

options.text: Switch quetiapine to lamotrigine for evidence-based BPD emotional regulation at this stage
options.rationale: No mood stabiliser has robust BPD evidence. The NHMRC guideline recommends against routine mood stabiliser use. The goal should be simplification, not substitution. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Cease all three simultaneously as none are evidence-based for BPD and ongoing monitoring of progress
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation is dangerous. Diazepam requires gradual taper (seizure risk), sertraline requires tapering (discontinuation syndrome), and sertraline has a legitimate MDD indication. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Gradually taper diazepam first, then review quetiapine, while maintaining sertraline for MDD relapse prevention
options.rationale: Priority is cessation of diazepam - benzodiazepines are cautioned in BPD due to disinhibition, dependence, and DBT skill interference. Quetiapine lacks long-term BPD evidence and carries metabolic risks. Sertraline is reasonable for MDD relapse prevention given only 8 months remission. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Increase quetiapine to 300 mg and add PRN benzodiazepine to replace scheduled diazepam
options.rationale: This escalates polypharmacy without evidence. PRN benzodiazepines carry the same BPD risks. Direction should be toward reduction. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; AMH)

explanation.summary: The NHMRC BPD guideline recommends regular medication review with a view to simplification. No medication is evidence-based for core BPD. Benzodiazepines are specifically cautioned. Comorbid depression may warrant continued antidepressant but BPD-specific prescribing should be minimised.
key_points: No medication is evidence-based for core BPD pathology
Benzodiazepines cautioned due to disinhibition and dependence
Regular review and simplification recommended
Comorbid MDD warrants continued antidepressant
Stability likely attributable to DBT not medication
explanation.pearls: NHMRC BPD guideline recommends maximum 12-week review for any BPD-targeted medication - if no clear benefit, taper and cease rather than continue indefinitely.

stem: A 33-year-old woman is referred for diagnostic clarification. She has emotional dysregulation, unstable relationships, and chronic emptiness. She experienced sustained childhood sexual abuse ages 6-12. She describes persistent negative self-concept, relationship difficulty due to distrust, and emotional numbing alternating with anger. She does not have impulsive self-harm, identity disturbance, or frantic abandonment avoidance. She has chronic hypervigilance and nightmares of the abuse.

data_table.History: CSA ages 6-12. No self-harm. No identity disturbance. No abandonment fears. Chronic hypervigilance and trauma nightmares.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best fits this presentation?

options.text: Borderline personality disorder given emotional dysregulation and interpersonal difficulties
options.rationale: She lacks core BPD features: no self-harm, no identity disturbance, no abandonment fears. Distrust-based interpersonal difficulties with negative self-concept following sustained trauma fit Complex PTSD better. (Source: ICD-11; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: PTSD with comorbid avoidant personality disorder explaining the distrust
options.rationale: AVPD features social inhibition and inadequacy feelings. Her difficulties stem from betrayal-trauma-based distrust rather than social anxiety. CPTSD better captures trauma-specific interpersonal disturbance. (Source: ICD-11; DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Comorbid BPD and PTSD as the conditions commonly co-occur after childhood trauma
options.rationale: Comorbid diagnosis requires meeting criteria for BOTH. She does not meet BPD criteria. ICD-11 specifically created CPTSD to capture presentations with emotion dysregulation from sustained trauma without requiring a personality disorder label. (Source: ICD-11)

options.text: Complex post-traumatic stress disorder with disturbances in self-organisation
options.rationale: ICD-11 CPTSD requires PTSD features (re-experiencing, avoidance, threat perception) PLUS disturbances in self-organisation: affect dysregulation, negative self-concept, interpersonal difficulties. She has all of these without BPD-specific features. (Source: ICD-11; RANZCP)

options.text: Adjustment disorder with mixed emotional features given the stressor chronicity
options.rationale: Adjustment disorder is time-limited and does not meet criteria for another disorder. This patient has chronic pervasive symptoms persisting 20+ years. Severity far exceeds adjustment disorder. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

explanation.summary: ICD-11 Complex PTSD is distinct from BPD. Key discriminators: BPD features abandonment fear, identity disturbance, and self-harm; CPTSD features distrust-based interpersonal difficulty, negative self-concept, and re-experiencing. Treatment implications differ - CPTSD responds to phased trauma therapy rather than DBT.
key_points: CPTSD = PTSD core + disturbances in self-organisation
BPD-specific features absent in CPTSD: identity disturbance, self-harm, abandonment
ICD-11 specifically distinguishes CPTSD from BPD
Interpersonal difficulties: CPTSD = distrust-based, BPD = abandonment-driven
Treatment for CPTSD is phased trauma therapy, not DBT
explanation.pearls: ICD-11 field trials demonstrated reliable clinical distinction between CPTSD and BPD - the key discriminator is whether interpersonal difficulties are driven by distrust (CPTSD) versus abandonment fear with frantic avoidance (BPD).

stem: A 27-year-old woman with BPD lives in regional NSW. She has recurrent self-harm and crises. No DBT program exists locally - the nearest is 3 hours away. She sees a GP fortnightly and has a Better Access psychologist offering general CBT. She will not travel for DBT. Her GP asks about locally deliverable evidence-based options.

data_table.History: BPD with recurrent self-harm. Regional NSW. No local DBT. Better Access psychologist available.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate locally deliverable treatment?

options.text: Standard CBT for BPD delivered by the Better Access psychologist with ongoing clinical monitoring
options.rationale: Standard CBT is not specifically effective for BPD. It does not adequately address emotion dysregulation or interpersonal patterns. BPD-specific approaches (DBT, MBT, schema therapy, SCM) are required. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Structured clinical management through her GP with psychoeducation, crisis planning, and consistent boundaries
options.rationale: SCM is evidence-based and deliverable by GPs without specialist DBT training. It involves psychoeducation, collaborative crisis planning, and consistent boundaries. The BOSCOT trial showed non-inferiority to specialist therapy for many outcomes. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline; Project Air Strategy)

options.text: Telehealth DBT delivered by a metropolitan therapist under Better Access at this stage
options.rationale: Standard DBT includes weekly skills group, consultation team, and phone coaching - difficult via telehealth for one isolated patient. Better Access typically provides 10-20 sessions annually, insufficient for 12-month DBT. SCM is more practical. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: Monthly psychiatrist telehealth with medication optimisation as primary treatment at this stage
options.rationale: Medication is not primary BPD treatment. No medication treats core pathology. Psychological intervention is primary. Monthly reviews without structured psychosocial approach would not address self-harm. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

options.text: ACT delivered by the local psychologist as a DBT alternative with appropriate specialist oversight
options.rationale: ACT has limited specific BPD evidence. SCM has stronger evidence as a generalist approach and is designed for non-specialist clinicians in resource-limited settings. (Source: NHMRC BPD Guideline)

explanation.summary: SCM is an evidence-based generalist approach deliverable by GPs and general clinicians. It involves psychoeducation, crisis planning, and consistent boundaries. Project Air Strategy provides free training. This addresses the access gap in regional Australia.
key_points: SCM is evidence-based and deliverable by generalist clinicians
Includes psychoeducation, crisis planning, consistent boundaries
Standard CBT alone insufficient for BPD
No medication is primary BPD treatment
Project Air provides free training resources
explanation.pearls: Project Air Strategy (University of Wollongong) provides free online SCM training for Australian clinicians managing BPD in generalist settings - equipping GPs without the 12-month DBT training requirement.

stem: A 21-year-old man is brought to the ED by police after being found running into traffic stating people were trying to kill him. He has been smoking high-potency cannabis daily for 3 years. Urine drug screen is positive for THC only. He is paranoid, disorganised, and responding to auditory hallucinations. This is his first psychotic presentation. He has no psychiatric history. His brother was diagnosed with schizophrenia at age 19.

data_table.Investigations: UDS positive THC only. BSL 6.2. Normal FBC, UEC, TFT, CRP.
data_table.History: Daily cannabis 3 years. First psychosis. Brother has schizophrenia. No prior psychiatric contact.

lead_in: What is the most important factor in distinguishing cannabis-induced psychosis from a primary psychotic disorder with cannabis comorbidity?

options.text: Resolution of psychotic symptoms within one month of cannabis cessation without ongoing antipsychotic requirement
options.rationale: The key distinguishing factor is temporal course. Cannabis-induced psychosis should resolve within 1 month of abstinence (DSM-5 requires resolution 'soon after' cessation). Persistent symptoms beyond this suggest a primary psychotic disorder unmasked by cannabis. This distinction requires longitudinal observation. (Source: DSM-5-TR; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; RANZCP Early Psychosis Guidelines)

options.text: Positive urine drug screen confirming recent cannabis use as the causative agent at this stage
options.rationale: Positive UDS confirms exposure but cannot determine causation. Cannabis use is extremely common in patients with primary psychotic disorders. THC positivity does not distinguish induced from primary psychosis. (Source: DSM-5-TR; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Absence of negative symptoms such as flat affect and social withdrawal at presentation
options.rationale: Negative symptoms can be absent in early presentations of primary psychosis (particularly in acute exacerbations) and can be present in cannabis-induced states (apathy, amotivation). Cross-sectional negative symptom assessment cannot distinguish the two. (Source: RANZCP Early Psychosis Guidelines)

options.text: Family history of schizophrenia confirming genetic predisposition to primary psychosis
options.rationale: Family history increases risk for primary psychosis but does not confirm the diagnosis in this episode. Cannabis-induced psychosis can still occur in those with family history. Conversely, family history may explain why cannabis triggered a primary disorder. It raises probability but is not diagnostic. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Predominantly visual rather than auditory hallucinations suggesting substance-induced aetiology
options.rationale: The assumption that substance-induced psychosis preferentially causes visual hallucinations is not reliably supported for cannabis specifically. Cannabis-induced psychosis commonly features paranoia and auditory hallucinations similar to primary psychosis. Modality alone is not a reliable discriminator. (Source: Lancet Psychiatry; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Distinguishing cannabis-induced from primary psychosis at first presentation is not possible cross-sectionally. The diagnosis is longitudinal: cannabis-induced psychosis resolves within approximately one month of abstinence. Persistent symptoms suggest primary psychosis unmasked by cannabis. Initial management is identical. Long-term follow-up is essential as 30-50% of cannabis-induced psychosis converts to a primary psychotic disorder.
key_points: Temporal course after cessation is the key discriminator
Cannabis-induced psychosis should resolve within 1 month of abstinence
Cross-sectional presentation cannot distinguish the two
Initial management is identical regardless of aetiology
30-50% of cannabis-induced psychosis converts to schizophrenia spectrum disorder
explanation.pearls: A meta-analysis showed 34% of cannabis-induced psychosis cases convert to schizophrenia within 3 years - the highest conversion rate of any substance-induced psychosis - making early follow-up and relapse prevention essential regardless of initial diagnostic impression.

stem: A 19-year-old man is brought to the ED by ambulance after smoking a substance purchased as 'herbal incense'. He is agitated, tachycardic, hypertensive, and vomiting. He has had two generalised tonic-clonic seizures en route. His friends report he smoked the product 90 minutes ago. Standard urine drug screen is negative for THC.

data_table.Investigations: UDS negative for all standard panels. BSL 7.8. VBG pH 7.31, lactate 4.2. CK 1840 U/L.

lead_in: What explains the negative THC result despite suspected cannabinoid exposure?

options.text: The patient likely consumed a different substance and is mistaken about what was smoked
options.rationale: While misidentification is possible, the combination of agitation, seizures, hypertension, tachycardia, and vomiting after smoking a marketed 'herbal' product is a classic synthetic cannabinoid toxidrome. The negative UDS is explained by assay limitations. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: The 90-minute interval is too short for THC metabolites to appear in urine at this stage
options.rationale: Natural THC metabolites are typically detectable in urine within 2-4 hours of smoking. While 90 minutes might produce a weak positive, the key issue is that synthetic cannabinoids are structurally distinct and their metabolites do not trigger the THC immunoassay regardless of timing. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Herbal incense products typically contain non-cannabinoid substances such as cathinones
options.rationale: While some products may be adulterated, 'herbal incense' or 'synthetic cannabis' products primarily contain synthetic cannabinoid receptor agonists. The presentation (seizures, hypertension, tachycardia) is consistent with synthetic cannabinoid toxicity which is more severe than natural cannabis. (Source: NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Synthetic cannabinoids are not detected by standard immunoassay THC screens which target delta-9-THC metabolites
options.rationale: Standard UDS immunoassays detect 11-nor-delta-9-THC-carboxylic acid (the metabolite of natural cannabis). Synthetic cannabinoids have different molecular structures and produce different metabolites not cross-reacting with standard assays. Specific mass spectrometry testing is required. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: The standard screen has insufficient sensitivity and would be positive if repeated in 6 hours
options.rationale: Repeating the standard immunoassay will not detect synthetic cannabinoids regardless of timing. The structural difference means these compounds never trigger the assay. Detection requires targeted LC-MS/MS testing for specific synthetic cannabinoid metabolites. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Poisons Information Centre)

explanation.summary: Synthetic cannabinoids are full agonists at CB1 receptors (natural THC is partial agonist), explaining their greater toxicity including seizures, rhabdomyolysis, and cardiovascular compromise. Standard UDS detects only natural THC metabolites. Synthetic cannabinoids require specific mass spectrometry testing. Management is supportive with benzodiazepines for seizures and agitation.
key_points: Synthetic cannabinoids are NOT detected by standard UDS immunoassays
They are full CB1 agonists (vs partial agonist THC) explaining greater toxicity
Seizures, rhabdomyolysis, AKI, and cardiac arrhythmias can occur
Detection requires LC-MS/MS for specific metabolites
Management is supportive: benzodiazepines, cooling, IV fluids
explanation.pearls: Synthetic cannabinoids are full CB1 receptor agonists with 10-800x greater binding affinity than delta-9-THC - this explains why toxicity (seizures, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis) far exceeds natural cannabis and why cross-reactivity with standard THC immunoassays does not occur.

stem: A 26-year-old man presents to the ED for the fifth time in 3 months with cyclical severe nausea and vomiting. Each episode lasts 24-48 hours and resolves spontaneously. He reports that prolonged hot showers provide temporary relief. He has used cannabis daily for 8 years. Previous workup including upper GI endoscopy, CT abdomen, and coeliac serology has been normal. Standard anti-emetics have been ineffective.

data_table.Investigations: Previous: OGD normal, CT abdomen normal, coeliac screen negative. This visit: Na 134 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, creatinine 108 micromol/L (baseline 82)
data_table.History: 5 ED presentations in 3 months. Daily cannabis 8 years. Hot shower relief.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate definitive management strategy?

options.text: Advise complete cannabis cessation as the only intervention proven to prevent recurrence
options.rationale: Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome (CHS) is caused by chronic cannabinoid exposure. Complete cannabis cessation is the only intervention proven to prevent recurrence. No pharmacotherapy prevents episodes while cannabis use continues. Hot water relief and cyclical pattern with chronic cannabis use are the diagnostic triad. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: GI; Gastroenterology literature)

options.text: Commence haloperidol 5 mg as a specific anti-emetic for cannabinoid hyperemesis episodes
options.rationale: While haloperidol and droperidol have evidence for symptomatic relief during acute CHS episodes, they do not prevent recurrence. The definitive treatment is cannabis cessation. Using haloperidol as ongoing management without addressing the cause perpetuates the condition. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: GI; Emergency Medicine Australasia)

options.text: Trial a proton pump inhibitor as the vomiting may represent atypical GORD despite normal endoscopy
options.rationale: Normal OGD with the classic CHS triad (cyclical vomiting, chronic cannabis use, hot water compulsion) does not warrant empiric PPI. The diagnosis is CHS. Treating an alternative diagnosis delays addressing the actual cause. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: GI)

options.text: Refer for capsaicin cream application to the abdomen as an evidence-based CHS-specific therapy
options.rationale: Topical capsaicin has been reported to provide symptomatic relief during acute episodes (via TRPV1 activation similar to hot water) but is not definitive management. It does not prevent recurrence. Cessation remains the only curative intervention. (Source: Emergency Medicine Australasia)

options.text: Prescribe ondansetron 8 mg TDS as prophylaxis between episodes at this stage
options.rationale: Standard anti-emetics including ondansetron are characteristically ineffective in CHS, which is a distinguishing feature from other causes of cyclical vomiting. Prophylactic ondansetron will not prevent episodes while cannabis use continues. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: GI; Gastroenterology)

explanation.summary: Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome occurs with chronic cannabis use and presents with cyclical severe vomiting, compulsive hot water bathing, and resolution with cessation. Standard anti-emetics are ineffective. The only definitive treatment is complete cannabis cessation. Acute symptomatic management includes IV fluids, capsaicin, and haloperidol/droperidol.
key_points: Complete cannabis cessation is the only proven prevention
Diagnostic triad: cyclical vomiting, chronic cannabis, hot water compulsion
Standard anti-emetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide) are characteristically ineffective
Haloperidol/droperidol provide acute symptomatic relief
Capsaicin cream provides temporary relief via TRPV1 activation
explanation.pearls: CHS typically occurs after 5+ years of daily cannabis use and resolves within 1-2 weeks of complete cessation - recurrence is near-universal if cannabis is resumed, even at lower doses than prior use.

stem: A 17-year-old boy is referred by his school counsellor for cannabis use. He smokes 3-4 times weekly for 18 months. His academic performance has declined from top-quartile to failing two subjects. He sleeps until midday on weekends. He does not believe cannabis is a problem and says all his friends use it. He is not interested in stopping but has agreed to see you once.

data_table.History: Cannabis 3-4x weekly, 18 months. Academic decline. Low motivation. Pre-contemplative regarding change.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate therapeutic approach for this single session?

options.text: Commence structured CBT for cannabis use with goal-setting and trigger identification
options.rationale: CBT techniques require the patient to have at least some motivation to change. In the pre-contemplative stage, CBT homework and goal-setting will not be completed. MI is needed first to build motivation before action-oriented strategies. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Provide psychoeducation about cannabis neurotoxicity in adolescent brain development
options.rationale: While accurate, unsolicited psychoeducation to a pre-contemplative patient is ineffective and risks therapeutic rupture. He has not asked for information and perceives no problem. Information provision should follow from self-identified concerns within MI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Motivational interviewing exploring discrepancy between his values and current functioning
options.rationale: For a pre-contemplative adolescent, motivational interviewing (MI) is the evidence-based approach. It explores discrepancy between values/goals and current behaviour without confrontation. A single MI session can shift readiness to change. Directive advice or CBT techniques are ineffective when the patient does not perceive a problem. (Source: RANZCP; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Recommend immediate abstinence and warn about academic consequences if use continues
options.rationale: Confrontational advice and warnings are counter-productive in pre-contemplative patients and provoke reactance. This approach damages the therapeutic alliance in the one session available and reduces likelihood of future help-seeking. (Source: RANZCP; Motivational Interviewing literature)

options.text: Refer to a residential adolescent drug treatment program given the severity of academic decline
options.rationale: Residential treatment is grossly disproportionate for 3-4x weekly cannabis use in a pre-contemplative teenager. It removes him from school and peers. Brief intervention with MI is first-line for early problematic use. Residential care is reserved for severe dependence with multiple failed community treatments. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Motivational interviewing is the evidence-based approach for pre-contemplative substance users, particularly adolescents. It explores discrepancy between values and behaviour without confrontation. Even a single MI session can shift readiness to change. Action-oriented techniques (CBT, goal-setting) are premature in pre-contemplation and will not engage the patient.
key_points: MI is first-line for pre-contemplative adolescent substance users
Explores discrepancy between values/goals and current behaviour
Non-confrontational approach preserves therapeutic alliance
CBT and goal-setting require at least some motivation to be effective
Single-session MI can shift stage of change
explanation.pearls: The FRAMES model for brief intervention (Feedback, Responsibility, Advice, Menu, Empathy, Self-efficacy) combined with a single motivational interviewing session produces measurable reduction in cannabis use at 3-month follow-up in adolescents, even without further sessions.

stem: A 22-year-old woman is brought to the ED from a music festival. She took two MDMA tablets 3 hours ago. She is agitated, diaphoretic, and confused. She is on sertraline 100 mg daily for anxiety but did not disclose this to her friends who provided the tablets.

data_table.Examination: Mydriasis, bilateral ankle clonus (>10 beats), hyperreflexia upper and lower limbs, diaphoresis, agitation
data_table.Investigations: Na 128 mmol/L, CK 2400 U/L, BSL 5.4

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Administer dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV for suspected neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: This is serotonin syndrome, not NMS. Key differentiators: rapid onset (hours vs days), clonus and hyperreflexia (absent in NMS), agitation (vs rigidity/bradykinesia), and serotonergic drug exposure. Dantrolene has no role in serotonin syndrome. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Administer cyproheptadine 12 mg orally stat as the specific serotonin syndrome antidote
options.rationale: Cyproheptadine is a serotonin antagonist used as an adjunct in moderate-severe serotonin syndrome but is NOT first-line. Cooling and benzodiazepines take priority. Additionally, cyproheptadine is only available orally and may not be absorbed in an agitated vomiting patient. It is second-line after supportive measures. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Aggressive cooling, IV benzodiazepines for agitation, and IV normal saline for hyponatraemia
options.rationale: This is serotonin syndrome precipitated by MDMA plus sertraline. Management priorities are cooling (target below 38.5), benzodiazepines for agitation and rigidity (reducing heat generation), and IV fluids. Hyponatraemia from SIADH/excess water intake needs careful correction. This is the standard toxicology approach per the Toxicology Handbook. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Poisons Information Centre)

options.text: Restrict fluids for the hyponatraemia and apply external cooling only if temperature exceeds 40 degrees
options.rationale: While hyponatraemia management may involve fluid restriction in some contexts, this patient with serotonin syndrome needs IV fluids for volume resuscitation and rhabdomyolysis prevention. Waiting until 40 degrees to cool is dangerous - active cooling should begin immediately at 39.8. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Administer 3% hypertonic saline for symptomatic hyponatraemia and intubate for airway protection
options.rationale: Hypertonic saline may be needed if the patient develops seizures from hyponatraemia (Na <120 or symptomatic), but at Na 128 with confusion likely from serotonin syndrome rather than hyponatraemia alone, aggressive sodium correction is premature. Intubation is reserved if sedation fails to control the syndrome. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Poisons Information Centre)

explanation.summary: MDMA combined with SSRIs precipitates serotonin syndrome. The classic triad is neuromuscular (clonus, hyperreflexia), autonomic (hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis), and mental status changes. Management priorities are cooling, benzodiazepines, and supportive care. MDMA also causes hyponatraemia via ADH release and excessive water intake. Cyproheptadine is second-line after supportive measures fail.
key_points: MDMA plus SSRI precipitates serotonin syndrome
Clonus and hyperreflexia distinguish from NMS
Cooling and benzodiazepines are first-line management
Hyponatraemia from SIADH/water intoxication is a common MDMA complication
Cyproheptadine is second-line, not first-line
explanation.pearls: MDMA-associated hyponatraemia results from both ADH release (SIADH) and excessive water intake in the setting of activity - Na below 125 mmol/L can cause seizures and cerebral oedema, but in the context of serotonin syndrome, the neuromuscular signs (clonus) help distinguish serotonergic toxicity from pure hyponatraemic encephalopathy.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents to the ED 8 hours after his last dose of GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate) which he has been using every 2-3 hours around the clock for 6 months. He is tremulous, diaphoretic, tachycardic, and increasingly agitated. He reports auditory hallucinations. He received diazepam 20 mg orally 2 hours ago with minimal effect. His GCS is 14. He is not responding to standard benzodiazepine doses used for alcohol withdrawal.

data_table.History: GHB q2-3h for 6 months. Last dose 8 hours ago. Minimal response to diazepam 20 mg.
data_table.Examination: Tremor, diaphoresis, agitation, responding to auditory hallucinations

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Administer phenobarbitone 15 mg/kg IV as GHB withdrawal does not respond to benzodiazepines
options.rationale: While phenobarbitone may be used as second-line, the statement that GHB withdrawal does not respond to benzodiazepines is incorrect - it responds to HIGH-DOSE benzodiazepines. Standard alcohol withdrawal doses are insufficient but escalated IV benzodiazepine dosing is first-line. Barbiturates are second-line adjuncts. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Escalate to high-dose IV diazepam titration in a monitored setting with ICU standby for potential intubation
options.rationale: GHB withdrawal is a medical emergency that can progress rapidly to delirium, seizures, and death. It is characteristically resistant to standard benzodiazepine doses used for alcohol withdrawal. High-dose IV benzodiazepine titration (often requiring 100-500 mg diazepam equivalent over 24 hours) in a monitored setting is required. ICU involvement is often necessary. Baclofen may be used as adjunct. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Health GHB Withdrawal Guidelines)

options.text: Commence baclofen 10 mg TDS as a specific GHB receptor agonist for withdrawal management
options.rationale: Baclofen (GABA-B agonist) has evidence as an adjunct in GHB withdrawal but is not sufficient as monotherapy for severe withdrawal with haemodynamic instability and hallucinations. High-dose benzodiazepines remain the primary agent. Baclofen oral dosing is also unreliable in an agitated patient. (Source: NSW Health GHB Guidelines; Toxicology Handbook)

options.text: Treat as alcohol withdrawal with CIWA-guided diazepam titration and thiamine supplementation
options.rationale: CIWA protocols are designed for alcohol withdrawal and use standard doses that are grossly inadequate for GHB withdrawal. GHB withdrawal requires much higher benzodiazepine doses and progresses faster than alcohol withdrawal. Treating it identically to alcohol withdrawal risks under-dosing and clinical deterioration. (Source: Toxicology Handbook; NSW Health GHB Guidelines)

options.text: Administer sodium oxybate (pharmaceutical GHB) as substitution therapy with planned taper
options.rationale: Sodium oxybate substitution is used in some European GHB detoxification protocols but is not standard practice in Australia and is not readily available in ED settings. In acute severe withdrawal with haemodynamic instability, high-dose benzodiazepine stabilisation is the immediate priority. Substitution therapy requires specialist addiction medicine input. (Source: Toxicology Handbook)

explanation.summary: GHB withdrawal is a life-threatening emergency that develops rapidly (within 1-6 hours of last dose in dependent users). It is characteristically resistant to standard benzodiazepine doses and can require 100-500+ mg diazepam equivalent over 24 hours. Delirium, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, and death can occur. ICU-level monitoring is often required. Baclofen and barbiturates are second-line adjuncts.
key_points: GHB withdrawal is life-threatening and progresses rapidly
Onset within 1-6 hours of last dose in around-the-clock users
Requires high-dose IV benzodiazepines far exceeding alcohol withdrawal protocols
ICU involvement often necessary for monitoring and potential intubation
Baclofen is an adjunct, not monotherapy for severe withdrawal
explanation.pearls: GHB withdrawal in around-the-clock users can require 200-1000 mg diazepam equivalent in the first 24 hours - the key clinical clue is withdrawal onset within hours (not days) and resistance to 'standard' benzodiazepine dosing, which should prompt immediate ICU escalation.

stem: A 45-year-old man with stable schizophrenia on clozapine 400 mg daily wants to quit smoking. He smokes 25 cigarettes per day. His previous quit attempt with NRT patches failed after 2 weeks. His clozapine level is 420 mcg/L (therapeutic range 350-600). He has no history of seizures. His psychiatrist is planning to prescribe varenicline.

data_table.Investigations: Clozapine level 420 mcg/L. FBC: neutrophils 4.2 x10^9/L.

lead_in: What critical pharmacokinetic interaction must be monitored during the quit attempt?

options.text: Varenicline directly inhibits clozapine metabolism requiring clozapine dose reduction before commencing
options.rationale: Varenicline does not inhibit CYP1A2 or any enzyme involved in clozapine metabolism. It has no direct pharmacokinetic interaction with clozapine. The critical interaction is between SMOKING CESSATION (regardless of method) and clozapine metabolism. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Smoking cessation will increase clozapine levels via reduced CYP1A2 induction requiring dose reduction
options.rationale: Cigarette smoke (not nicotine) induces CYP1A2 which metabolises clozapine. When smoking stops, CYP1A2 activity decreases over 3-7 days, raising clozapine levels by 50-70%. At baseline level 420, cessation could push levels above 600 causing toxicity (seizures, sedation, cardiac effects). Proactive dose reduction of 25-50% with monitoring is required. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Varenicline lowers seizure threshold synergistically with clozapine requiring prophylactic anticonvulsant
options.rationale: The EAGLES trial demonstrated no increased neuropsychiatric adverse effects with varenicline in psychiatric patients. While both clozapine and varenicline have theoretical seizure associations, the combination does not require prophylactic anticonvulsant. The real risk is from clozapine toxicity due to level rise. (Source: AMH; EAGLES trial)

options.text: Nicotine replacement therapy must be ceased before starting varenicline due to nicotinic receptor antagonism
options.rationale: Varenicline is a partial nicotinic receptor agonist, not antagonist. Combination therapy (varenicline plus NRT) has been studied and is sometimes used. Additionally, this patient failed NRT alone. The question is about clozapine interaction, not NRT co-prescribing. (Source: AMH)

options.text: Clozapine must be switched to an alternative antipsychotic before a quit attempt due to toxic interaction risk
options.rationale: Switching from clozapine (which may be the only effective antipsychotic for this patient) is not necessary. The interaction is manageable with proactive dose reduction and level monitoring during the quit attempt. Many patients on clozapine successfully quit smoking with appropriate pharmacokinetic management. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Cigarette smoke induces CYP1A2, the primary enzyme metabolising clozapine and olanzapine. When smoking ceases, enzyme induction reverses over 3-7 days, causing clozapine levels to rise by 50-70%. This is a smoking cessation effect, not a varenicline interaction. Proactive clozapine dose reduction (25-50%) with weekly level monitoring is essential during any quit attempt.
key_points: Smoke (not nicotine) induces CYP1A2 metabolising clozapine
Cessation raises clozapine levels 50-70% within one week
Proactive dose reduction of 25-50% required during quit attempts
Varenicline has no direct pharmacokinetic interaction with clozapine
Monitor clozapine levels weekly for first 4 weeks of cessation
explanation.pearls: Clozapine levels rise by approximately 50-70% within 7 days of smoking cessation via loss of CYP1A2 induction - the dose reduction should be proactive (start reducing on quit day), not reactive (after toxicity signs appear), with weekly level checks for the first month.

stem: A 52-year-old woman wants to quit smoking (20 cigarettes/day for 30 years). She has tried varenicline previously but developed nausea requiring cessation. She has no contraindications to NRT. She asks about the most effective NRT approach. She has a history of well-controlled hypertension on amlodipine.

data_table.History: 20 cig/day, 30 years. Varenicline intolerant (nausea). Hypertension on amlodipine.

lead_in: What is the most effective NRT strategy for this patient?

options.text: 4 mg nicotine gum alone as oral forms are more effective than patches for heavy smokers
options.rationale: While 4 mg gum is appropriate for heavy smokers, oral NRT alone is not more effective than patch alone. The most effective strategy is combination (patch plus oral). Gum alone requires regular disciplined use and misses the steady-state baseline coverage provided by patches. (Source: Cochrane Review NRT; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: 21 mg nicotine patch alone as combination NRT increases cardiovascular risk in hypertensive patients
options.rationale: Combination NRT does not significantly increase cardiovascular risk in patients with controlled hypertension. NRT is far safer than continued smoking. The cardiovascular caution applies to acute coronary syndromes within 2 weeks, not stable hypertension. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: 21 mg nicotine patch daily combined with 2 mg nicotine gum as needed for breakthrough cravings
options.rationale: Combination NRT (long-acting patch for baseline coverage plus short-acting oral form for breakthrough) is more effective than either alone. Meta-analyses show combination NRT approaches the efficacy of varenicline. For a 20/day smoker, 21 mg patch is appropriate starting dose with oral top-up. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic; Cochrane Review NRT)

options.text: Start with 14 mg patch and uptitrate to 21 mg if ineffective to minimise nicotine exposure
options.rationale: For a 20/day smoker, 21 mg is the appropriate starting dose. Starting at 14 mg under-doses the patient and increases the risk of early relapse from inadequate craving control. Conservative dosing is not justified - the goal is adequate replacement of nicotine to prevent relapse. (Source: AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Nicotine mouth spray alone as it provides the fastest onset matching cigarette pharmacokinetics
options.rationale: While mouth spray provides faster nicotine delivery than gum or lozenge, using it alone without a patch provides only intermittent coverage. Combination therapy with a patch for baseline plus spray for breakthrough would be appropriate, but spray alone is inferior to combination therapy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: Combination NRT (patch plus short-acting oral form) is the most effective NRT strategy. The patch provides steady-state baseline nicotine while oral forms manage breakthrough cravings. Meta-analyses show combination NRT approaches varenicline efficacy. Controlled hypertension is not a contraindication to NRT.
key_points: Combination NRT (patch + oral) is more effective than either alone
Efficacy approaches that of varenicline
21 mg patch appropriate for 20+ cigarettes/day
Controlled hypertension is not a contraindication
Combination NRT is safe in cardiovascular disease
explanation.pearls: Combination NRT (21 mg patch plus ad libitum 2-4 mg gum or lozenge) achieves 6-month abstinence rates of 25-30%, approaching varenicline monotherapy (28-33%) and significantly exceeding single-form NRT (15-20%).

stem: A 28-year-old woman who smokes 15 cigarettes daily presents at 12 weeks gestation. She has attempted behavioural counselling alone for 4 weeks without success. She asks about pharmacotherapy. She has no other substance use and no psychiatric history.

data_table.History: 15 cig/day. 12 weeks pregnant. Failed behavioural counselling 4 weeks.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy option?

options.text: 21 mg nicotine patch continuously as it provides the most consistent nicotine replacement
options.rationale: In pregnancy, intermittent NRT forms are preferred over continuous patch delivery. The patch provides constant nicotine exposure to the foetus across 24 hours including sleep. Intermittent forms allow periods of lower foetal nicotine exposure and overall lower total daily dose. (Source: COPE Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Varenicline as it has the highest cessation efficacy and no confirmed teratogenicity
options.rationale: Varenicline is not recommended in pregnancy due to insufficient safety data. While no confirmed teratogenicity exists, the precautionary principle applies as human pregnancy data is very limited. NRT has a much longer safety track record in pregnancy. (Source: AMH; TGA; COPE Guidelines)

options.text: Bupropion as it avoids nicotine exposure entirely while supporting cessation
options.rationale: Bupropion is not recommended as first-line in pregnancy. While it avoids nicotine exposure, pregnancy safety data is limited. NRT is preferred as the pregnancy-specific pharmacotherapy after counselling failure. (Source: AMH; COPE Guidelines)

options.text: Continue behavioural counselling only as no pharmacotherapy is safe in pregnancy
options.rationale: This is incorrect - NRT is recommended when behavioural intervention alone fails. Continued smoking causes more harm than NRT (IUGR, preterm birth, placental abruption, stillbirth). After 4 weeks of failed counselling, escalation to NRT is appropriate. (Source: COPE Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: NRT intermittent-use forms such as gum or lozenge after discussing risks and benefits with the patient
options.rationale: In pregnancy, NRT is the only pharmacotherapy recommended after behavioural counselling fails. Intermittent forms (gum, lozenge) are preferred over continuous patch as they deliver less total nicotine. The risk-benefit discussion acknowledges that NRT is safer than continued smoking but not risk-free. Australian guidelines support NRT in pregnancy after counselling failure. (Source: COPE Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

explanation.summary: In pregnancy, NRT is the only recommended pharmacotherapy after behavioural counselling fails. Intermittent forms (gum, lozenge) are preferred over patches as they deliver less total nicotine to the foetus. The decision requires informed risk-benefit discussion. Continued smoking is more harmful than NRT. Varenicline and bupropion are not recommended in pregnancy.
key_points: NRT is the only pharmacotherapy recommended in pregnancy
Intermittent forms preferred over continuous patch
Behavioural counselling is first-line but NRT appropriate when it fails
Continued smoking is more harmful than NRT to the foetus
Varenicline and bupropion not recommended in pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Intermittent NRT (gum or lozenge) in pregnancy delivers approximately 30-60% less total daily nicotine than continued smoking and allows foetal nicotine-free periods during sleep - the patch is second-line due to continuous 24-hour foetal exposure.

stem: A 48-year-old man wants to quit smoking (30/day). He has tried NRT patches and gum (combination) with relapse at 3 weeks, and varenicline which he ceased after 1 week due to vivid nightmares and insomnia. He asks about bupropion. His medical history includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes on metformin and a single generalised seizure 8 years ago after heavy alcohol use (no recurrence, not on anticonvulsants, drinks 2 standard drinks per week now).

data_table.History: 30 cig/day. Failed combination NRT (relapse 3wk). Varenicline intolerant (nightmares). T2DM on metformin. Single alcohol-related seizure 8 years ago.

lead_in: Is bupropion appropriate for this patient?

options.text: Yes - the alcohol-related context means it was not a true epileptic seizure so the contraindication does not apply
options.rationale: The TGA contraindication language does not distinguish aetiology. Any seizure history (provoked, unprovoked, febrile, alcohol-related) triggers the contraindication. Bupropion should not be prescribed regardless of the seizure's context or cause. (Source: AMH; TGA Product Information)

options.text: Yes - a single provoked seizure from alcohol 8 years ago without recurrence does not constitute a seizure disorder
options.rationale: The TGA contraindication for bupropion is 'any history of seizures' - this includes both provoked and unprovoked seizures, single and recurrent. The contraindication does not distinguish between seizure disorders and single provoked events. Bupropion lowers seizure threshold dose-dependently. (Source: AMH; TGA Product Information)

options.text: Yes - provided an EEG is normal and no anticonvulsant is required, bupropion can be used at reduced dose
options.rationale: A normal EEG does not override the seizure history contraindication. There is no dose-reduction pathway that makes bupropion acceptable in patients with seizure history. The contraindication is absolute regardless of EEG findings or current seizure-free status. (Source: AMH; TGA Product Information)

options.text: No - bupropion is contraindicated due to his history of seizure regardless of the interval or context
options.rationale: Bupropion is contraindicated in patients with any history of seizure disorder or any seizure of any cause. This is an absolute contraindication in the TGA product information. Even a single provoked seizure 8 years ago from alcohol constitutes a seizure history. Alternative approaches include re-trialling NRT at higher doses or with different oral forms. (Source: AMH; TGA Product Information Zyban)

options.text: No - but the reason is the diabetes, not the seizure history, as bupropion increases hypoglycaemia risk with metformin
options.rationale: Diabetes on metformin is not a contraindication to bupropion. While bupropion may rarely affect blood glucose, this is not a standard contraindication. The actual contraindication is the seizure history. (Source: AMH)

explanation.summary: Bupropion is absolutely contraindicated in patients with any history of seizures - regardless of aetiology, context, time interval, or recurrence. This includes alcohol-provoked seizures. Bupropion lowers seizure threshold in a dose-dependent manner. For this patient, options include re-trialling combination NRT with different oral forms, cytisine, or addressing barriers to previous NRT relapse.
key_points: Bupropion is contraindicated with ANY seizure history
Contraindication applies regardless of aetiology (alcohol, drug, febrile, unprovoked)
No dose reduction or EEG clearance pathway exists
Alternative: re-trial NRT with different approach, cytisine
Bupropion lowers seizure threshold dose-dependently (risk approximately 1/1000)
explanation.pearls: Bupropion seizure risk is approximately 1 in 1000 at standard dose (150 mg BD) but increases to 1 in 100 at doses above 450 mg/day - the absolute contraindication applies to any seizure history because even standard doses lower threshold sufficiently to provoke events in susceptible individuals.

stem: A 34-year-old woman is referred for ASD assessment. She reports lifelong difficulty with social relationships despite appearing socially competent. She describes exhaustion after social interactions, having learned to mimic facial expressions and small talk through conscious observation. She has intense interests in true crime and textile arts. She was a high-achieving student. She has been diagnosed with GAD, social anxiety, and depression over the years. Her daughter was recently diagnosed with ASD at age 6.

data_table.History: Previous diagnoses: GAD, social anxiety, MDD. Childhood: high-achieving but no friends. Conscious social mimicry. Daughter diagnosed ASD age 6.

lead_in: What clinical feature most strongly supports ASD rather than social anxiety disorder as the primary diagnosis?

options.text: Intense special interests in true crime and textiles distinguishing ASD from anxiety disorders
options.rationale: While intense interests are characteristic of ASD, they alone are not sufficient for diagnosis and can overlap with normal variation. Many neurotypical people have intense hobbies. The key diagnostic weight is in the social communication domain combined with restricted interests. (Source: DSM-5-TR; National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Comorbid generalised anxiety and depression supporting the neurodevelopmental theory of ASD
options.rationale: GAD and depression are common comorbidities of both ASD and SAD. Their presence does not distinguish between the two conditions. They may represent downstream consequences of undiagnosed ASD but are not diagnostically specific. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: High academic achievement which rules out social anxiety as SAD impairs academic performance
options.rationale: High academic achievement does not rule out SAD - many people with SAD perform well academically (particularly in non-oral assessment). Academic success also does not confirm ASD. It is the NATURE of social difficulty (mimicry vs avoidance) that distinguishes the conditions. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Family history of ASD in her daughter confirming genetic predisposition at this stage
options.rationale: Family history raises pre-test probability but does not confirm diagnosis. The daughter's diagnosis should prompt assessment but is not itself a diagnostic feature of ASD in the mother. Clinical features must independently meet criteria. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Conscious effortful mimicry of neurotypical social behaviour resulting in post-social exhaustion
options.rationale: Camouflaging/masking - the conscious effort to perform neurotypical social behaviour through learned scripts and mimicry - is a hallmark feature of ASD in women that distinguishes it from social anxiety. In SAD, avoidance is driven by fear of negative evaluation. In masked ASD, social behaviour is possible but requires conscious cognitive effort resulting in fatigue. (Source: RANZCP; National Guideline for Assessment and Diagnosis of ASD in Australia)

explanation.summary: ASD in women is frequently missed or misdiagnosed as anxiety disorders because masking/camouflaging conceals social communication differences. The key discriminator is that ASD masking involves CONSCIOUS COGNITIVE EFFORT to perform social behaviour, resulting in exhaustion, whereas SAD involves AVOIDANCE driven by fear. Women with ASD often receive multiple prior psychiatric diagnoses before correct identification.
key_points: Masking/camouflaging is conscious effortful mimicry of neurotypical behaviour
Post-social exhaustion from masking is characteristic of ASD not SAD
Women with ASD average 4-5 prior psychiatric diagnoses before correct identification
Childhood social history reveals differences even if compensated by intelligence
Family history of ASD in offspring should prompt parental assessment
explanation.pearls: Women with ASD are diagnosed on average 4-5 years later than men, with a mean of 3-4 prior psychiatric diagnoses - the key screening question is whether social competence requires conscious effort and scripting versus coming naturally, which distinguishes masked ASD from social anxiety.

stem: A 28-year-old man with ASD Level 1 (previously Asperger syndrome) presents to the ED with his support worker. He was in a shopping centre when the fire alarm sounded and he is now crouched in the corner, rocking, hands over ears, and non-verbal. He was physically uninjured by the alarm. He is not responding to verbal instructions. His support worker reports this has happened before and typically resolves within 30-60 minutes in a quiet environment. His vital signs are normal.

data_table.History: ASD Level 1. Known sensory overload episodes. Triggered by fire alarm. Non-verbal, rocking, hands over ears.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ED management?

options.text: Arrange psychiatric consultation for acute psychotic episode given his non-verbal state and abnormal behaviour
options.rationale: This is not psychosis. The support worker provides context - this is a known pattern of sensory overload response. He has ASD and this presentation is consistent with autistic shutdown. No psychiatric emergency exists. Misidentifying sensory overload as psychosis leads to inappropriate treatment. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Administer midazolam 5 mg IM for acute behavioural disturbance as he is non-responsive at this stage
options.rationale: This is not acute behavioural disturbance - he is not aggressive, combative, or posing a risk to others. He is in sensory shutdown. Sedation is inappropriate and potentially harmful, as it does not address the cause and delays natural recovery. Non-verbal rocking is a self-regulation strategy. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia; NSW Health ABD Guidelines)

options.text: Provide a quiet low-stimulation space, reduce sensory input, allow time, and follow the support worker's guidance on his needs
options.rationale: This is autistic shutdown/meltdown from sensory overload, not a psychiatric emergency. Management is environmental modification: quiet space, dim lights, minimal verbal demands, reduce sensory input, and allow self-regulation time. The support worker knows his patterns. No medication or restraint is needed. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia; RANZCP)

options.text: Attempt verbal de-escalation using calm clear instructions and eye contact to re-orient him at this stage
options.rationale: Verbal demands and eye contact during sensory overload increase distress. The correct approach is to REDUCE stimulation, not add more. Verbal instructions should be minimised. If communication is needed, visual or written cues are preferred. Forcing eye contact is counterproductive. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Perform a CT brain to exclude acute neurological cause of his sudden non-verbal state at this stage
options.rationale: The context clearly explains this presentation - known ASD with known sensory overload pattern, triggered by fire alarm, witnessed by support worker who confirms this is typical. CT brain is not indicated for a characteristic sensory shutdown with a clear precipitant and normal vitals. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

explanation.summary: Autistic shutdown from sensory overload is a protective response, not a psychiatric emergency. Management is environmental: provide quiet space, reduce stimulation, minimise demands, and allow self-regulation. Support workers are the best guide to individual needs. Sedation, psychiatric referral, and investigations are inappropriate for a known pattern with clear trigger.
key_points: Sensory overload shutdown is not a psychiatric emergency
Reduce stimulation: quiet space, dim lights, minimal verbal demands
Support worker guidance is invaluable for individual needs
Sedation is inappropriate and harmful
Verbal de-escalation and eye contact INCREASE distress during overload
explanation.pearls: Autistic shutdown (withdrawal, non-verbal, rocking) differs from meltdown (outward distress, crying, aggression) but both result from sensory or cognitive overload - both resolve with time and reduced stimulation, and neither requires pharmacological intervention unless there is comorbid risk to self or others.

stem: A 30-year-old software developer with ASD Level 1 is referred by his GP for mental health review. He is highly skilled technically but has received performance warnings for not attending team meetings, avoiding client phone calls, and wearing noise-cancelling headphones at his desk which his manager finds rude. He describes the open-plan office as overwhelming. He is not depressed or anxious beyond workplace distress. He asks what medical letter he should request for his employer.

data_table.History: ASD Level 1. Software developer. Performance warnings for social avoidance and headphone use. No depression/anxiety beyond workplace.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate recommendation?

options.text: Suggest he find a different career better suited to his social communication profile and review clinical response at follow-up at this stage
options.rationale: He is highly skilled in his technical role. The issues are environmental (open-plan, mandatory meetings, phone calls) not competency-based. The Disability Discrimination Act requires reasonable adjustment rather than career change. Many workplaces successfully accommodate autistic employees. (Source: Disability Discrimination Act 1992)

options.text: Recommend he commence an SSRI for social anxiety to improve his workplace social functioning with regular monitoring of clinical parameters
options.rationale: His social difficulties are not from anxiety disorder - they are from ASD-related sensory sensitivity and social communication differences. An SSRI will not change his sensory processing or social communication neurology. The appropriate intervention is environmental adjustment, not medication for a non-existent anxiety disorder. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Provide a letter recommending reasonable workplace adjustments under the Disability Discrimination Act including modified meeting participation and sensory accommodations
options.rationale: ASD is a disability under the Disability Discrimination Act 1992 (Cth). Employers have a legal obligation to provide reasonable adjustments. Appropriate accommodations include alternative meeting formats (written updates, video-off), quiet workspace or permission for noise-cancelling headphones, and modified client contact arrangements. A treating doctor's letter supports the formal request. (Source: Disability Discrimination Act 1992; Australian Human Rights Commission; National ASD Guideline)

options.text: Refer for social skills training to improve his workplace interpersonal performance with close clinical monitoring throughout at this stage
options.rationale: While social skills training may be helpful as one component, it does not address the sensory environment issues and places the burden of adaptation entirely on the employee. Workplace accommodations addressing the environment are more appropriate and are a legal right. The headphones are an accommodation, not rudeness. (Source: National ASD Guideline Australia)

options.text: Advise him that ASD does not qualify as a disability under employment law as he is high-functioning with appropriate specialist oversight
options.rationale: This is incorrect. ASD at any level is a disability under the DDA. The term 'high-functioning' is outdated and does not negate disability status. Support needs exist on a spectrum and Level 1 ASD with workplace impairment qualifies for reasonable adjustments. (Source: Disability Discrimination Act 1992; Australian Human Rights Commission)

explanation.summary: ASD is a disability under Australian law regardless of level. Employers must provide reasonable adjustments. Common workplace accommodations include modified sensory environments, alternative communication formats, flexible meeting participation, and adjusted social expectations. Medical documentation supports formal requests.
key_points: ASD is covered by the Disability Discrimination Act 1992
Employers must provide reasonable adjustments
Common accommodations: quiet space, headphones, written communication, modified meetings
Level 1/'high-functioning' does not negate disability status
The burden of adjustment should not rest solely on the employee
explanation.pearls: Under the Disability Discrimination Act 1992, employers must provide reasonable adjustments unless they can demonstrate 'unjustifiable hardship' - common ASD workplace accommodations (quiet workspace, noise-cancelling headphones, asynchronous communication) rarely meet this threshold.

stem: A 31-year-old woman with schizophrenia well controlled on quetiapine 600 mg daily presents at 8 weeks gestation in a planned pregnancy. Her previous psychotic relapse (when she ceased medication independently) required 3 months inpatient admission. She and her partner have discussed the risks and want to continue medication. She asks which antipsychotic has the most pregnancy safety data.

data_table.History: Schizophrenia, one relapse requiring 3-month admission on medication cessation. Planned pregnancy.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate medication advice?

options.text: Reduce quetiapine to the minimum effective dose of 200 mg to minimise foetal exposure at this stage
options.rationale: Arbitrary dose reduction without evidence of efficacy at lower doses risks relapse. Her stable dose is 600 mg and her last relapse on no medication required 3 months inpatient. Dose should only be reduced if monitoring shows she is stable at a lower dose, not empirically reduced based on pregnancy alone. (Source: COPE Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to haloperidol as typical antipsychotics have the longest overall pregnancy safety record
options.rationale: While haloperidol has long-term use data in pregnancy, switching antipsychotics during pregnancy introduces instability risk. COPE guidelines recommend continuing the effective agent rather than switching solely for theoretical safety advantage. The relapse risk from switching outweighs any marginal safety difference. (Source: COPE Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: Continue quetiapine as it has the most pregnancy exposure data among atypical antipsychotics and she is stable on it
options.rationale: Quetiapine has the largest pregnancy registry data among atypical antipsychotics (along with olanzapine) and no confirmed teratogenicity signal. COPE guidelines recommend continuing the effective antipsychotic rather than switching in pregnancy. Switching risks relapse during the transition and introduces a new agent with potentially less safety data. (Source: COPE Guidelines; AMH; TGA)

options.text: Cease quetiapine and manage with intensive psychological support throughout pregnancy at this stage
options.rationale: Psychotic relapse in pregnancy carries significant risks: self-harm, poor nutrition, substance use, medication non-adherence, and disrupted antenatal care. Her relapse history (3 months inpatient) demonstrates severe illness on cessation. Psychological support alone cannot prevent psychotic relapse. (Source: COPE Guidelines; RANZCP)

options.text: Switch to aripiprazole as it has the lowest metabolic side effects benefiting both mother and foetus
options.rationale: Lower metabolic effects do not equate to greater pregnancy safety. Switching introduces relapse risk and aripiprazole has less pregnancy exposure data than quetiapine. The principle is to continue the effective agent in pregnancy rather than switching for theoretical advantages. (Source: COPE Guidelines; AMH)

explanation.summary: COPE guidelines recommend continuing the effective antipsychotic in pregnancy rather than switching. Quetiapine and olanzapine have the most atypical antipsychotic pregnancy exposure data without confirmed teratogenicity. Switching risks relapse during a vulnerable period. Relapse in pregnancy carries significant risks for both mother and foetus.
key_points: Continue the effective antipsychotic - do not switch in pregnancy
Quetiapine and olanzapine have the most pregnancy exposure data
Switching antipsychotics risks relapse during vulnerable period
Relapse in pregnancy carries significant maternal and foetal risks
Dose reduction should only occur if clinically safe, not empirically
explanation.pearls: Quetiapine and olanzapine each have over 1000 first-trimester exposures in pregnancy registries without a consistent teratogenicity signal - COPE recommends continuing whichever antipsychotic achieves stability rather than switching to a theoretically 'safer' agent.

stem: A 26-year-old primigravida at 32 weeks gestation scores 14 on the Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale (EPDS) administered by her midwife at routine antenatal visit. She endorsed item 10 (self-harm thoughts) as 'sometimes'. She appears tearful but denies active suicidal intent or plan. She has no psychiatric history and no substance use.

data_table.Screening: EPDS total 14/30. Item 10 (self-harm thoughts): 'Yes, quite often' (score 2/3).
data_table.History: Primigravida, 32 weeks. No psychiatric history. Supportive partner.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Immediate psychiatric emergency assessment given endorsement of self-harm thoughts in pregnancy at this stage
options.rationale: While item 10 endorsement requires follow-up, EPDS is a screening tool not a diagnostic instrument. Endorsing 'sometimes' having self-harm thoughts without intent or plan does not mandate emergency psychiatric assessment. It requires further clinical assessment by the referring clinician to determine severity and urgency. (Source: COPE Guidelines; beyondblue Perinatal Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the EPDS in two weeks as a single elevated score may represent normal pregnancy adjustment at this stage
options.rationale: An EPDS score of 14 with positive self-harm item cannot be dismissed as adjustment. The threshold for further assessment is 13+ (possible depression) or any positive score on item 10. Waiting two weeks without intervention when self-harm thoughts are endorsed is not safe practice. (Source: COPE Guidelines)

options.text: Discuss item 10 further to assess risk, provide immediate psychosocial support, and arrange GP or perinatal mental health review within one week
options.rationale: COPE guidelines recommend that any endorsement of EPDS item 10 triggers further clinical assessment of suicidal ideation. This should occur immediately (by the administering clinician or GP) to determine severity. An EPDS score of 13+ indicates probable depression requiring follow-up. Referral to perinatal mental health services within 1-2 weeks is appropriate when acute risk is excluded. (Source: COPE Guidelines; beyondblue Perinatal Guidelines)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily as EPDS above 13 indicates antenatal depression requiring pharmacotherapy at this stage
options.rationale: EPDS is a screening tool, not diagnostic. A score above 13 indicates probable depression requiring clinical assessment, not automatic medication. Diagnosis requires clinical interview. If confirmed, first-line for mild-moderate antenatal depression is psychological therapy, with medication for moderate-severe. (Source: COPE Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Psychotropic)

options.text: Document the score and discuss at the next antenatal visit in 4 weeks as the midwife cannot initiate referral
options.rationale: Midwives can and should initiate referral based on EPDS results. Waiting 4 weeks with a positive self-harm item is unsafe. COPE guidelines empower all perinatal clinicians (midwives, GPs, obstetricians) to act on EPDS results including referral. (Source: COPE Guidelines)

explanation.summary: EPDS scores of 13+ indicate probable depression requiring clinical assessment. Any endorsement of item 10 (self-harm thoughts) requires immediate further assessment of suicidal ideation regardless of total score. The EPDS is a screening tool, not diagnostic - clinical interview must follow. Management pathways range from psychosocial support to specialist referral depending on assessed severity.
key_points: EPDS 13+ = probable depression requiring assessment
Item 10 endorsement always requires immediate further risk assessment
EPDS is a screening tool, not diagnostic - clinical assessment must follow
Midwives and all perinatal clinicians can initiate referral
Pharmacotherapy requires confirmed diagnosis, not just elevated screening score
explanation.pearls: EPDS item 10 endorsement of any score above 0 triggers an immediate brief risk assessment by the administering clinician - this does not require emergency referral in most cases but does require same-day clinical evaluation, safety planning, and timely mental health follow-up within 1-2 weeks.

stem: A 28-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of fever, pharyngitis, diffuse maculopapular rash, myalgia, and cervical lymphadenopathy. He reports unprotected receptive anal intercourse with a new male partner 3 weeks ago. His rapid HIV antibody point-of-care test is negative.

data_table.Investigations: Rapid HIV Ab test: negative. FBC: lymphocytes 0.8 x10^9/L (low). ALT 68 U/L.
data_table.Examination: Diffuse maculopapular rash trunk and limbs, bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, pharyngeal erythema, no exudate

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Fourth-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination assay and HIV RNA viral load
options.rationale: This presentation is classic acute HIV seroconversion (mononucleosis-like illness 2-4 weeks after exposure). Rapid antibody tests miss acute infection as antibodies take 3-12 weeks to develop. Fourth-generation assays detect p24 antigen (positive from 2 weeks) alongside antibodies. HIV RNA PCR is positive earliest (from 10 days). Both should be sent when acute seroconversion is suspected. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Repeat rapid HIV antibody test in 2 weeks to allow time for seroconversion
options.rationale: Waiting 2 weeks delays diagnosis of a highly infectious phase. During acute seroconversion viral load is extremely high (10^6-10^7 copies/mL) making the patient maximally infectious. Early diagnosis enables partner notification, PEP for contacts, and early ART which improves long-term outcomes. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: EBV and CMV serology as the presentation is consistent with infectious mononucleosis
options.rationale: While the presentation overlaps with EBV/CMV, the 3-week interval after high-risk sexual exposure makes acute HIV seroconversion the primary diagnosis to exclude. Sending EBV/CMV without HIV-specific testing risks missing a treatable and transmissible infection. HIV testing must be prioritised. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: HIV Western blot as the definitive confirmatory test for equivocal rapid results
options.rationale: Western blot is a confirmatory test for ESTABLISHED infection, not acute seroconversion. In acute infection, Western blot may be negative or indeterminate as individual antibody bands develop sequentially. The appropriate tests are p24 antigen and viral load, not Western blot. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: CD4 count and viral load to stage the infection before initiating treatment
options.rationale: CD4 count and viral load are used for staging established infection, but the diagnosis must first be confirmed. A CD4 count alone cannot diagnose HIV. The fourth-generation Ag/Ab assay is the diagnostic test. Staging follows after confirmed diagnosis. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Acute HIV seroconversion presents as a mononucleosis-like illness 2-4 weeks after exposure. Rapid antibody tests are negative during the window period. Fourth-generation Ag/Ab combination assays detect p24 antigen from 2 weeks, closing much of the window period. HIV RNA PCR becomes positive earliest. During acute infection, viral load is extremely high making the patient maximally infectious.
key_points: Rapid Ab tests miss acute seroconversion - use 4th-gen Ag/Ab + RNA
p24 antigen detectable from approximately 2 weeks post-exposure
HIV RNA detectable from approximately 10 days
Acute seroconversion viral load is 10^6-10^7 - maximally infectious
Early diagnosis enables partner notification and early ART
explanation.pearls: Fourth-generation HIV Ag/Ab combination assays reduce the window period to approximately 18 days (vs 3 months for antibody-only tests), but HIV RNA PCR closes it further to approximately 10 days - in suspected acute seroconversion, both should be ordered simultaneously.

stem: A 35-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with HIV after presenting with oral candidiasis and weight loss. Her initial workup is completed and ART is being commenced.

data_table.Investigations: CD4 count 85 cells/microL, HIV viral load 180,000 copies/mL. Toxoplasma IgG positive. CMV IgG positive. TB QuantiFERON negative. HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive.

lead_in: Which opportunistic infection prophylaxis regimen should be commenced alongside ART?

options.text: TMP-SMX for PJP plus valganciclovir for CMV prophylaxis given positive CMV serology and severe immunosuppression
options.rationale: CMV prophylaxis with valganciclovir is not recommended for primary prophylaxis in HIV. CMV disease prophylaxis is considered only at CD4 <50, and even then, universal prophylaxis is not standard practice - regular fundoscopy for CMV retinitis is preferred. CMV IgG positivity indicates past exposure, not active disease. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole plus azithromycin 1200 mg weekly for MAC prophylaxis at this stage
options.rationale: MAC prophylaxis with azithromycin is indicated at CD4 <50. Her CD4 is 85, above this threshold. Adding azithromycin would be unnecessary medication with potential side effects and drug interactions. TMP-SMX alone covers the relevant pathogens at her CD4 level. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: No prophylaxis required as ART initiation alone will restore CD4 count and prevent opportunistic infections
options.rationale: While ART will restore immune function, the time to CD4 recovery above prophylaxis thresholds is typically 3-6 months. During this vulnerable period, the patient remains at risk. Prophylaxis bridges the gap until immune reconstitution occurs. It can be ceased once CD4 is sustained above threshold on ART. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Dapsone 100 mg daily as first-line PJP prophylaxis plus pyrimethamine for toxoplasmosis cover
options.rationale: Dapsone plus pyrimethamine is an alternative for patients intolerant of TMP-SMX. TMP-SMX is first-line due to superior efficacy, dual PJP/toxoplasmosis coverage in a single agent, and additional benefit against some bacterial infections. Dapsone is reserved for sulfonamide allergy. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg daily for both PJP and toxoplasmosis prophylaxis
options.rationale: At CD4 <200, PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX is indicated. At CD4 <100 with positive Toxoplasma IgG, toxoplasmosis prophylaxis is also indicated - TMP-SMX at double-strength daily dose covers both. Her CD4 of 85 triggers both thresholds. MAC prophylaxis is not needed as CD4 is above 50. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: OI prophylaxis in HIV is guided by CD4 thresholds. PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) at CD4 <200, toxoplasmosis prophylaxis at CD4 <100 if seropositive, and MAC prophylaxis at CD4 <50. TMP-SMX covers both PJP and toxoplasmosis. Prophylaxis continues until CD4 is sustained above the relevant threshold on ART, typically after 3-6 months.
key_points: PJP prophylaxis: CD4 <200 - TMP-SMX first-line
Toxoplasmosis prophylaxis: CD4 <100 + positive IgG - TMP-SMX double-strength
MAC prophylaxis: CD4 <50 - azithromycin
CMV: monitor with fundoscopy at CD4 <50, no universal prophylaxis
Cease prophylaxis when CD4 sustained above threshold for 3-6 months on ART
explanation.pearls: TMP-SMX double-strength daily simultaneously covers PJP prophylaxis (CD4 <200) and toxoplasmosis prophylaxis (CD4 <100 with positive IgG) - the dose for dual coverage is 160/800 mg daily, compared to 80/400 mg or thrice-weekly for PJP-only prophylaxis.

stem: A 40-year-old man with newly diagnosed HIV (CD4 45, VL 320,000) was commenced on ART and TMP-SMX prophylaxis 3 weeks ago. He also had latent TB treated with isoniazid commenced 2 weeks before ART. He now presents with worsening fevers, new cervical lymphadenopathy with fluctuance, and a new right middle lobe consolidation on chest X-ray. His CD4 has risen to 120 and viral load has dropped to 2,500.

data_table.Investigations: CD4 120 (was 45), VL 2,500 (was 320,000). CRP 142. CXR: RML consolidation.
data_table.Examination: Right cervical lymphadenopathy, fluctuant, tender. New cough.

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: ART treatment failure with progression of AIDS-defining tuberculosis at this stage
options.rationale: ART treatment failure would show RISING viral load and FALLING CD4. This patient shows the opposite: VL has dropped from 320,000 to 2,500 and CD4 has risen from 45 to 120. This pattern confirms virological response to ART, not failure. The worsening clinical picture paradoxically accompanies immunological improvement. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome unmasking subclinical TB despite isoniazid prophylaxis
options.rationale: This is classic unmasking TB-IRIS. He was treated for latent TB but may have had undiagnosed subclinical active TB. As ART restored immune function (CD4 rise, VL drop), the recovering immune system mounted an inflammatory response against previously subclinical mycobacterial disease. New lymphadenopathy and consolidation in the context of rising CD4 on ART is the hallmark presentation. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Community-acquired pneumonia unrelated to HIV as his improving immune status reduces infection susceptibility
options.rationale: While CAP is possible, the combination of new lymphadenopathy, consolidation, and clinical deterioration in the context of recent ART initiation with rising CD4 strongly suggests IRIS rather than a new community-acquired infection.
