The fluctuant lymphadenopathy is particularly suggestive of mycobacterial IRIS. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

options.text: Isoniazid-resistant TB that has broken through prophylaxis requiring second-line treatment
options.rationale: While drug-resistant TB should be considered, the timing (3 weeks after ART with improving viral/immunological parameters) points to IRIS as the mechanism. Drug resistance would be expected to present independently of immune reconstitution. Drug susceptibility testing should be performed on any cultured organisms but IRIS remains the most likely explanation. (Source: ASHM HIV Management Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Drug reaction to ART presenting as a systemic inflammatory response at this stage
options.rationale: ART hypersensitivity (particularly abacavir) can cause fever and systemic symptoms, but new focal consolidation and fluctuant lymphadenopathy are not typical drug reaction features. The pattern is organ-specific (lung and nodes) consistent with IRIS against a mycobacterial antigen. (Source: AMH; ASHM HIV Management Guidelines)

explanation.summary: IRIS occurs when recovering immune function on ART generates an exaggerated inflammatory response against previously subclinical or treated opportunistic infections. TB-IRIS is the most common form, presenting as new or worsening lymphadenopathy, consolidation, or effusions despite virological response to ART. It typically occurs 1-8 weeks after ART initiation, particularly when baseline CD4 is very low.
key_points: IRIS occurs with IMPROVING immunological parameters (rising CD4, falling VL)
TB-IRIS is the most common form - new symptoms despite ART response
Risk factors: very low baseline CD4, high VL, starting ART close to OI treatment
Management: continue ART, treat TB, add corticosteroids if severe
Distinguish from ART failure (rising VL) and drug resistance
explanation.pearls: TB-IRIS risk is highest when ART is commenced within 2 weeks of TB treatment initiation and baseline CD4 is below 50 - the PredART trial showed that prednisone 40 mg daily for 2 weeks then 20 mg for 2 weeks reduces TB-IRIS incidence by 30% in high-risk patients.

stem: A 25-year-old man who has sex with men presents requesting HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP). He reports inconsistent condom use with casual partners and a recent diagnosis of rectal gonorrhoea treated 6 weeks ago. His 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab test today is negative. He has a hepatitis B surface antigen positive result with HBeAg positive and HBV DNA of 28,000 IU/mL. His renal function shows eGFR 92.

data_table.Investigations: HIV 4th-gen Ag/Ab: negative. HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, HBV DNA 28,000 IU/mL. eGFR 92. STI screen: pharyngeal and rectal GC/CT negative.
data_table.History: MSM. Inconsistent condoms. Recent rectal gonorrhoea.

lead_in: What critical consideration must be addressed before commencing tenofovir-emtricitabine PrEP?

options.text: The recent gonorrhoea diagnosis requires a repeat STI screen to confirm clearance before PrEP can start
options.rationale: While STI screening is part of routine PrEP assessment, treated gonorrhoea 6 weeks ago does not require test-of-cure before PrEP initiation. Current STI screen is negative. PrEP should not be delayed for this reason when the patient is at ongoing high risk. (Source: ASHM PrEP Guidelines)

options.text: Active hepatitis B is an absolute contraindication to tenofovir-emtricitabine PrEP and he should use condoms only
options.rationale: Active HBV is not an absolute contraindication to PrEP. Tenofovir-emtricitabine provides dual benefit (HIV prevention and HBV treatment). The critical issue is management of cessation risk, not prohibition. Denying PrEP to a high-risk individual due to a manageable concern is not best practice. (Source: ASHM PrEP Guidelines)

options.text: His eGFR of 92 is below the threshold for safe tenofovir use requiring renal referral before commencing
options.rationale: eGFR 92 is well above the standard PrEP threshold. Tenofovir disoproxil PrEP is generally safe with eGFR above 60 (some guidelines use >50). eGFR monitoring every 3-6 months is recommended during PrEP but initiation at eGFR 92 is appropriate. (Source: ASHM PrEP Guidelines; AMH)

options.text: His chronic hepatitis B requires gastroenterology review as tenofovir cessation could trigger a severe HBV flare
options.rationale: Tenofovir disoproxil-emtricitabine (the standard PrEP regimen) is also active against HBV. In a patient with chronic active hepatitis B (HBeAg+, high viral load), commencing and then discontinuing PrEP could trigger a severe hepatitis B flare from loss of antiviral suppression. He needs gastroenterology/hepatology review to: (1) assess if HBV treatment is independently indicated, (2) counsel about flare risk on PrEP cessation, (3) establish monitoring. PrEP can proceed but with informed consent about this risk and a plan for HBV management if PrEP is ever stopped. (Source: ASHM PrEP Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: GI)

options.text: On-demand (event-driven) PrEP rather than daily dosing should be prescribed to reduce cumulative tenofovir renal toxicity
options.rationale: On-demand PrEP is an option for MSM, but in this case with active HBV, daily dosing is actually preferred as it provides continuous HBV suppression. Event-driven dosing would create intermittent HBV suppression/rebound cycles, potentially worsening hepatic outcomes. Daily tenofovir-emtricitabine serves dual HIV prevention and HBV treatment purposes. (Source: ASHM PrEP Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: GI)

explanation.summary: In patients with chronic hepatitis B starting tenofovir-containing PrEP, the critical concern is HBV flare on cessation. Tenofovir-emtricitabine suppresses HBV replication. If PrEP is discontinued, viral rebound can trigger severe hepatitis flare potentially leading to hepatic decompensation. This does not preclude PrEP but requires hepatology involvement, informed consent, and a HBV management plan for any future PrEP cessation.
key_points: Tenofovir-emtricitabine PrEP treats HBV concurrently - this is beneficial
HBV flare risk on PrEP cessation is the critical concern
Hepatology review recommended before starting - not a contraindication
Daily PrEP preferred over on-demand in HBV co-infection
Monitor HBV DNA and LFTs during and after any PrEP changes
explanation.pearls: Tenofovir-emtricitabine PrEP cessation in HBsAg-positive patients can trigger HBV flares within 2-8 weeks, with ALT elevations up to 10x ULN reported - all PrEP prescribers must check HBsAg before initiation and involve hepatology if positive.

stem: A 42-year-old man with untreated HIV (diagnosed today, CD4 32, VL 450,000) presents with 2 weeks of progressive headache, confusion, and low-grade fevers. He has no focal neurological signs. CT brain shows no mass lesion or hydrocephalus. Lumbar puncture opening pressure is 32 cmH2O.

data_table.Investigations: CSF: protein 0.62 g/L, glucose 2.1 mmol/L (serum 5.8), WCC 12 (predominantly lymphocytes). India ink preparation shows encapsulated yeast. Cryptococcal antigen titre CSF 1:2048.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Voriconazole as it has superior CNS penetration compared to amphotericin B for fungal meningitis and review clinical response at follow-up
options.rationale: Voriconazole is not recommended for cryptococcal meningitis. While it has antifungal activity, it is less effective than amphotericin B plus flucytosine for this indication. Voriconazole is primarily used for aspergillosis. The established induction regimen (amphotericin + flucytosine) has the strongest evidence base. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; ASHM)

options.text: High-dose fluconazole 1200 mg daily as induction therapy with mannitol for raised intracranial pressure with appropriate dose adjustment
options.rationale: Fluconazole monotherapy, even at high dose, is inferior to amphotericin B plus flucytosine for induction and is associated with higher mortality. Mannitol does not effectively manage cryptococcal ICP - therapeutic LPs are the evidence-based approach as the ICP is caused by impaired CSF reabsorption, not cerebral oedema. (Source: ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Amphotericin B and flucytosine induction with immediate ART initiation to accelerate immune recovery and ongoing monitoring of progress
options.rationale: Immediate ART initiation in cryptococcal meningitis increases mortality through IRIS. The COAT trial demonstrated that early ART (within 1-2 weeks) versus delayed ART (5 weeks) significantly increased mortality. ART should be delayed 4-6 weeks until CSF is sterilised and clinical stability is achieved. (Source: ASHM; COAT trial; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Liposomal amphotericin B with dexamethasone to reduce meningeal inflammation and intracranial pressure and review clinical response accordingly
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are CONTRAINDICATED in HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis. The evidence shows no benefit and potential harm from adjunctive dexamethasone (unlike bacterial meningitis where dexamethasone is beneficial). ICP management is through therapeutic LPs, not steroids. (Source: ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: IV amphotericin B deoxycholate plus flucytosine for at least 2 weeks induction, with daily therapeutic lumbar punctures to manage raised intracranial pressure
options.rationale: Cryptococcal meningitis management follows a structured protocol: 2-week induction with amphotericin B plus flucytosine, followed by consolidation with fluconazole 400 mg daily for 8 weeks, then maintenance fluconazole 200 mg daily. Raised intracranial pressure (>25 cmH2O) requires daily therapeutic LPs to reduce pressure by draining CSF until opening pressure is consistently below 20 cmH2O. ART should be DELAYED 4-6 weeks to reduce IRIS risk. (Source: ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis requires a structured treatment approach: induction with amphotericin B plus flucytosine for 2 weeks, consolidation with fluconazole for 8 weeks, then long-term maintenance. Raised ICP is managed with daily therapeutic LPs, not steroids or mannitol. ART initiation must be DELAYED 4-6 weeks to reduce IRIS-related mortality. The COAT trial demonstrated increased mortality with early ART in cryptococcal meningitis.
key_points: Induction: amphotericin B + flucytosine for 2 weeks minimum
Daily therapeutic LPs for opening pressure >25 cmH2O
Corticosteroids are contraindicated in cryptococcal meningitis
DELAY ART for 4-6 weeks to reduce IRIS mortality
Consolidation: fluconazole 400 mg for 8 weeks, then maintenance 200 mg
explanation.pearls: The COAT trial (NEJM 2014) randomised cryptococcal meningitis patients to early ART (1-2 weeks) versus delayed ART (5 weeks) - early ART increased mortality by 15% absolute, establishing the standard of delaying ART 4-6 weeks until CSF sterilisation is confirmed.

stem: A 30-year-old man from Myanmar who arrived in Australia 6 months ago as a refugee presents for routine health assessment. He is asymptomatic with no cough, fevers, or weight loss. He received BCG vaccination as an infant. His chest X-ray is normal. His QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus test returns positive.

data_table.Investigations: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus: positive. CXR: normal. FBC normal. LFTs normal.
data_table.History: Myanmar-born, BCG vaccinated. Asymptomatic. Arrived Australia 6 months ago.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Treat latent TB with isoniazid 300 mg daily for 9 months with pyridoxine supplementation
options.rationale: Positive QuantiFERON in an asymptomatic patient with normal CXR indicates latent TB infection (LTBI). IGRA (QuantiFERON) is preferred over TST in BCG-vaccinated individuals as it is not affected by prior BCG. Treatment of LTBI is indicated in this high-risk patient (refugee from high-burden country). Standard regimens include isoniazid for 9 months or rifampicin for 4 months. Pyridoxine prevents isoniazid-induced peripheral neuropathy. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: No treatment needed as a positive QuantiFERON in a BCG-vaccinated individual is likely a false positive
options.rationale: QuantiFERON (IGRA) uses antigens (ESAT-6, CFP-10) not present in BCG. Therefore, BCG vaccination does NOT cause false-positive IGRA results. A positive QuantiFERON in a BCG-vaccinated person indicates true M. tuberculosis infection. This is precisely why IGRA is preferred over TST in BCG-vaccinated populations. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence full 4-drug RIPE therapy as positive QuantiFERON with recent migration suggests active TB
options.rationale: Positive IGRA with normal CXR and no symptoms indicates LTBI, not active TB. Full RIPE therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) is for active TB only. Over-treating LTBI with 4-drug therapy exposes the patient to unnecessary drug toxicity. Active TB requires clinical features or radiological/microbiological evidence. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Obtain three early-morning sputum samples for AFB smear and culture before any treatment decision
options.rationale: Sputum sampling for active TB is indicated when there are symptoms or abnormal CXR suggesting active disease. In an asymptomatic patient with normal CXR, sputum sampling is not indicated. The diagnosis of LTBI is based on positive IGRA with exclusion of active disease clinically and radiologically. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Observe without treatment and repeat QuantiFERON in 12 months as LTBI reactivation risk is low in young adults
options.rationale: LTBI reactivation risk is lifelong and estimated at 5-10% overall, with highest risk in the first 2 years after infection and in immunosuppressive states. Refugees from high-burden countries are a priority group for LTBI treatment. Delaying treatment without indication is not recommended when the patient is eligible and willing. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: LTBI screening with IGRA (QuantiFERON) is preferred in BCG-vaccinated individuals as it avoids BCG-related false positives. Positive IGRA with normal CXR and no symptoms indicates LTBI requiring treatment. Standard regimens include isoniazid for 9 months or rifampicin for 4 months. Refugees from high-burden countries are a priority population for LTBI treatment.
key_points: IGRA is preferred over TST in BCG-vaccinated individuals
QuantiFERON uses ESAT-6/CFP-10 antigens not present in BCG
LTBI: positive IGRA + normal CXR + no symptoms
Treatment: isoniazid 9 months or rifampicin 4 months
Pyridoxine 25 mg daily prevents isoniazid neuropathy
explanation.pearls: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus measures IFN-gamma responses to ESAT-6 and CFP-10 antigens which are absent from all BCG strains and most non-tuberculous mycobacteria - this gives it >95% specificity in BCG-vaccinated populations compared to 56-60% for TST.

stem: A 55-year-old man born in India presents with 6 weeks of productive cough, 5 kg weight loss, drenching night sweats, and low-grade fevers. He migrated to Australia 20 years ago. He is a taxi driver. Chest X-ray shows right upper lobe cavitating consolidation.

data_table.Investigations: CXR: right upper lobe cavitating consolidation with air-fluid level. Sputum AFB smear: positive (2+). HIV test: negative.
data_table.History: Born India. Productive cough 6 weeks, weight loss, night sweats.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate action alongside commencing anti-TB treatment?

options.text: Wait for culture confirmation before notifying public health as AFB smear alone cannot confirm M. tuberculosis
options.rationale: While AFB smear detects acid-fast bacilli (which include NTM), in the clinical context of cavitary disease with compatible symptoms, smear-positive disease is treated as TB until proven otherwise. Notification should not be delayed for culture (which takes 2-8 weeks). Public health units are notified on clinical suspicion with smear positivity. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Commence treatment and arrange outpatient follow-up as he is clinically stable and can self-isolate at home
options.rationale: Smear-positive cavitary TB requires hospital-based respiratory isolation until he is rendered non-infectious (typically after 2 weeks of effective treatment with clinical improvement and three negative sputum smears). A taxi driver with cavitary TB poses a public health risk. Home isolation is insufficient for smear-positive disease with high bacillary load. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; NSW Health TB Protocol)

options.text: Notify the state public health unit, isolate the patient in a negative-pressure room, and initiate contact tracing
options.rationale: TB is a notifiable disease in all Australian states and territories. Smear-positive cavitary TB is highly infectious. Immediate actions include: respiratory isolation (negative-pressure room with N95 masks for staff), notification to public health unit within 24 hours, and initiation of contact tracing including close household and occupational contacts (taxi passengers are generally not traced unless prolonged exposure). (Source: NTAC Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NSW Health TB Protocol)

options.text: Start treatment but defer contact tracing until drug susceptibility results are available to inform screening approach
options.rationale: Contact tracing should commence immediately, not wait for DST (which takes 4-8 weeks for culture-based methods). Contacts need screening regardless of drug susceptibility. DST results may later inform treatment decisions for the index case but do not affect the urgency of identifying exposed contacts. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Commence treatment and notify his GP as the reporting authority for communicable diseases
options.rationale: TB notification goes to the state/territory public health unit, not just the GP. In most jurisdictions, both the treating doctor and the laboratory have independent notification obligations. The public health unit coordinates contact tracing, directly observed therapy, and ensures treatment completion. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; NSW Health TB Protocol)

explanation.summary: Smear-positive pulmonary TB requires immediate notification to the public health unit, respiratory isolation in a negative-pressure room, and contact tracing. TB is notifiable in all Australian jurisdictions. Cavitary smear-positive disease is highly infectious. Contact tracing begins immediately and does not wait for culture or susceptibility results. The public health unit coordinates the response.
key_points: TB is notifiable to state public health unit within 24 hours of suspicion
Smear-positive cavitary TB requires negative-pressure isolation
Contact tracing begins immediately, not after culture/DST
Non-infectiousness: typically after 2 weeks treatment + clinical improvement + negative smears
Notification obligation is on treating doctor AND laboratory independently
explanation.pearls: Smear-positive cavitary TB has a bacterial load of 10^7-10^9 organisms per mL of sputum compared to 10^3-10^5 in smear-negative disease - cavitary patients are 5-10x more infectious and require strict respiratory isolation until rendered non-infectious.

stem: A 60-year-old woman commenced on standard RIPE therapy (rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol) for pulmonary TB presents at week 3 with nausea, vomiting, and right upper quadrant discomfort. She was well before treatment. She drinks no alcohol.

data_table.Investigations: ALT 380 U/L (baseline 22), AST 310 U/L (baseline 18), ALP 95 U/L, bilirubin 48 micromol/L, INR 1.1. HBsAg negative, HCV Ab negative.
data_table.Medications: Rifampicin 600 mg, isoniazid 300 mg, pyrazinamide 1500 mg, ethambutol 800 mg (all daily). Pyridoxine 25 mg daily.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reduce doses of all four drugs by 50% and monitor LFTs weekly while continuing treatment with appropriate dose adjustment
options.rationale: Dose reduction does not adequately address drug-induced hepatotoxicity at this severity (ALT 17x ULN with symptoms). The standard approach is cessation of hepatotoxic agents, not dose reduction. Sub-therapeutic dosing also risks drug resistance development. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Cease all hepatotoxic agents (isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide), continue ethambutol, and reintroduce sequentially once LFTs normalise
options.rationale: ALT >5x ULN with symptoms constitutes drug-induced hepatotoxicity requiring cessation of all potentially hepatotoxic agents. Pyrazinamide is the most hepatotoxic, followed by isoniazid, then rifampicin. Ethambutol is not hepatotoxic and can continue. Sequential reintroduction after LFT normalisation (typically rifampicin first, then isoniazid, then pyrazinamide last or omitted) allows identification of the causative agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Cease pyrazinamide only as it is the most hepatotoxic agent and continue the other three drugs at this stage
options.rationale: While pyrazinamide is the most hepatotoxic, with ALT 17x ULN, all potentially hepatotoxic agents should be ceased. Continuing isoniazid and rifampicin with this degree of hepatotoxicity risks progression to fulminant liver failure. Sequential reintroduction identifies the specific causative agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Continue all drugs as transient transaminase elevation is expected and self-limiting in the first month
options.rationale: Mild asymptomatic transaminase elevation (ALT <3x ULN) can be monitored without cessation. However, ALT 380 (17x ULN) with symptoms (nausea, vomiting, RUQ pain) exceeds all thresholds for continued treatment. This is significant hepatotoxicity requiring drug cessation. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Switch to a fluoroquinolone-based regimen (moxifloxacin, ethambutol, injectable aminoglycoside) permanently
options.rationale: A permanent switch to second-line agents is premature before attempting sequential reintroduction. Many patients can tolerate reintroduced rifampicin and isoniazid - pyrazinamide is most commonly the culprit and can be omitted (extending treatment to 9 months). Second-line regimens are reserved for patients who cannot tolerate any first-line hepatotoxic agent. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Anti-TB drug hepatotoxicity occurs in 5-10% of patients on RIPE therapy. ALT >5x ULN (or >3x with symptoms) mandates cessation of hepatotoxic agents. The standard approach is to cease isoniazid, rifampicin, and pyrazinamide, continue ethambutol (non-hepatotoxic), and sequentially reintroduce once LFTs normalise. Rifampicin is typically reintroduced first, then isoniazid, then pyrazinamide last.
key_points: Cease hepatotoxic agents if ALT >5x ULN or >3x with symptoms
Pyrazinamide > isoniazid > rifampicin in hepatotoxicity risk
Ethambutol is not hepatotoxic and can continue
Sequential reintroduction: rifampicin first, then isoniazid, then pyrazinamide
If pyrazinamide cannot be reintroduced, extend total treatment to 9 months
explanation.pearls: When pyrazinamide is permanently omitted due to hepatotoxicity, the intensive phase shortens to 2 months of rifampicin plus isoniazid plus ethambutol, but the total treatment duration extends from 6 to 9 months to compensate for the lost sterilising activity of pyrazinamide.

stem: A 38-year-old man born in Papua New Guinea presents with smear-positive pulmonary TB. He was treated for TB in PNG 3 years ago but reports completing only 3 months of a 6-month course due to medication access issues. GeneXpert MTB/RIF performed on his sputum detects M. tuberculosis with rifampicin resistance.

data_table.Investigations: Sputum AFB smear positive (3+). GeneXpert MTB/RIF: M. tuberculosis detected, rifampicin resistance detected. HIV negative.
data_table.History: Born PNG. Previous incomplete TB treatment (3 of 6 months). Current presentation: cough 8 weeks, haemoptysis, weight loss 8 kg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Start RIPE plus moxifloxacin as a five-drug intensive regimen while awaiting full DST results
options.rationale: Simply adding moxifloxacin to RIPE is not adequate MDR-TB treatment. If rifampicin is resistant, the rifampicin component is ineffective. If isoniazid is also resistant (likely in MDR-TB), the regimen collapses to pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and moxifloxacin - suboptimal. MDR-TB requires specifically designed regimens under specialist guidance. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; WHO MDR-TB Guidelines)

options.text: Commence standard RIPE therapy as GeneXpert rifampicin resistance may be a false positive at this stage
options.rationale: GeneXpert has >95% sensitivity and >98% specificity for rifampicin resistance. In a patient with previous incomplete treatment from a high MDR-TB burden country (PNG), the pre-test probability is very high making a false positive extremely unlikely. Starting standard RIPE with rifampicin-resistant TB would be ineffective, risking further resistance development and clinical deterioration. (Source: NTAC Guidelines)

options.text: Commence an MDR-TB regimen under specialist guidance and refer to the state TB service for ongoing management with DOT
options.rationale: GeneXpert rifampicin resistance in a patient with previous incomplete treatment strongly suggests MDR-TB (resistance to at least rifampicin and isoniazid). This requires specialist infectious diseases/respiratory physician management at a designated TB service. Treatment is complex (typically 9-20 month regimen with fluoroquinolone, bedaquiline, linezolid, and other agents), requires directly observed therapy (DOT), and close monitoring. Culture and full DST must be sent but should not delay empiric MDR treatment. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; NTAC MDR-TB Guidelines)

options.text: Wait for full phenotypic drug susceptibility testing before commencing any treatment to tailor the regimen
options.rationale: Phenotypic DST takes 4-8 weeks from culture. Waiting this long before treating smear-positive TB with rifampicin resistance risks clinical deterioration and ongoing transmission. Empiric MDR-TB treatment should begin immediately based on GeneXpert results while awaiting full DST to refine the regimen. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: Re-treat with the same RIPE regimen for a full 6-month course as the previous failure was due to non-adherence not resistance
options.rationale: While non-adherence caused the initial treatment failure, incomplete treatment is the primary driver of acquired drug resistance. GeneXpert has confirmed rifampicin resistance. Re-treating with RIPE would be ineffective against resistant organisms and would risk amplifying resistance to remaining drugs. (Source: NTAC Guidelines; WHO MDR-TB Guidelines)

explanation.summary: GeneXpert rifampicin resistance in a patient with previous incomplete TB treatment strongly indicates MDR-TB. Management requires specialist TB service involvement, empiric MDR-TB regimen (not standard RIPE), directly observed therapy, and isolation. Modern shorter MDR-TB regimens (BPaL - bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) have improved outcomes. Full DST guides regimen refinement but should not delay empiric treatment.
key_points: GeneXpert rifampicin resistance = MDR-TB until proven otherwise
Incomplete previous treatment is the strongest risk factor for drug resistance
MDR-TB treatment requires specialist TB service and DOT
Do not wait for full DST - start empiric MDR regimen immediately
PNG is a high MDR-TB burden country bordering Australia
explanation.pearls: The BPaL regimen (bedaquiline, pretomanid, linezolid) for MDR-TB has achieved cure rates exceeding 90% in the TB-PRACTECAL trial with only 6 months of treatment - a paradigm shift from the previous 18-24 month injectable-based regimens with 50-60% cure rates.

stem: A 32-year-old woman from Zimbabwe is diagnosed with both smear-positive pulmonary TB and HIV (CD4 38, VL 520,000). She has commenced standard RIPE therapy. The clinical team is deciding when to start ART. She has no signs of cryptococcal disease and her serum CrAg screen is negative.

data_table.Investigations: CD4 38 cells/microL, VL 520,000. CrAg screen negative. Sputum AFB smear positive. TB drug susceptibility: pending.

lead_in: What is the optimal timing for ART initiation in this patient?

options.text: Within 2 weeks of commencing TB treatment given her profoundly low CD4 count at this stage
options.rationale: Multiple RCTs (SAPIT, CAMELIA, STRIDE) demonstrate that early ART (within 2 weeks of TB treatment) reduces mortality in patients with CD4 <50 compared to delayed ART (8-12 weeks). The mortality benefit outweighs the IRIS risk at this level of immunosuppression. IRIS is managed if it occurs rather than avoided by delaying essential treatment. The exception is TB meningitis and cryptococcal meningitis where ART is delayed. (Source: ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic; SAPIT trial)

options.text: After completing the 2-month intensive phase of TB treatment to reduce drug interaction complexity
options.rationale: Waiting 2 months in a patient with CD4 38 increases mortality. While drug interactions exist (particularly rifampicin with protease inhibitors and some integrase inhibitors), these are manageable. Dolutegravir at doubled dose (50 mg BD instead of daily) or efavirenz at standard dose can be used with rifampicin. (Source: ASHM; SAPIT trial)

options.text: After 8 weeks of TB treatment regardless of CD4 count to minimise IRIS risk at this stage
options.rationale: Delayed ART for IRIS prevention was studied in the SAPIT and CAMELIA trials and found to increase mortality at CD4 <50. The mortality reduction from early ART exceeds the morbidity from IRIS. Delayed ART is only appropriate when CD4 is >50, or in TB meningitis or concurrent cryptococcal meningitis. (Source: ASHM; CAMELIA trial)

options.text: Simultaneously with TB treatment initiation on day 1 to maximise survival benefit
options.rationale: Same-day initiation of ART and TB treatment is not recommended. Starting both simultaneously makes it difficult to distinguish drug side effects and intolerances between the two regimens. The standard recommendation for CD4 <50 is within 2 weeks (not day 1) of TB treatment to allow initial TB drug tolerance assessment. (Source: ASHM; Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic)

options.text: After TB drug susceptibility results confirm drug-sensitive TB to avoid ART-RIPE interaction complications
options.rationale: Waiting for DST (4-8 weeks) delays ART by an unacceptable duration in a patient with CD4 38. ART should be started within 2 weeks regardless of pending DST. If DST later reveals resistance, the TB regimen is modified, but this does not affect ART timing. (Source: ASHM; NTAC Guidelines)

explanation.summary: In TB-HIV co-infection with CD4 <50, ART should begin within 2 weeks of TB treatment. This is based on landmark trials (SAPIT, CAMELIA, STRIDE) showing mortality reduction despite increased IRIS risk. The exception is TB meningitis where early ART increases mortality. Drug interactions (rifampicin with ARTs) are manageable - dolutegravir dose is doubled to 50 mg BD with rifampicin.
key_points: CD4 <50: ART within 2 weeks of TB treatment start
CD4 >50: ART can be deferred to 8 weeks after TB treatment
Exception: TB meningitis - delay ART 4-8 weeks
Rifampicin + dolutegravir: increase dolutegravir to 50 mg BD
IRIS is managed if it occurs, not avoided by delaying ART
explanation.pearls: The SAPIT trial showed that among TB-HIV patients with CD4 <50, early ART (within 4 weeks of TB treatment) reduced mortality by 56% compared to delayed ART (after intensive phase) - the NNT to prevent one death was 6, establishing early ART as one of the highest-impact interventions in HIV medicine.
